description: The dusky shark is a large, wide-ranging requiem shark found in tropical and warm temperate waters worldwide. It undertakes extensive coastal migrations and is one of the slowest-maturing and slowest-reproducing of all large sharks, making populations especially vulnerable to fishing pressure. Despite protections in some regions, populations have declined by over 80% in parts of its range.
funFact: Dusky sharks have one of the slowest life cycles of any shark — females don't reach maturity until age 20, have a 22–24 month gestation period, and reproduce only every 3 years — meaning populations can take decades to recover from even modest depletion.
habitat: Coastal and offshore waters in tropical and warm temperate regions worldwide. Found from inshore bays and estuaries to the open ocean at depths of 0–400 m. Undertakes pronounced north-south seasonal migrations along coastlines.
diet: A highly opportunistic predator feeding on a wide variety of bony fish, smaller sharks and rays, squid, octopus, and crustaceans. Uses its broadly curved jaw and serrated teeth to take large prey items, and is known to follow prey schools on seasonal migrations.
size: Typically 2.5–3.2 m (8.2–10.5 ft); max confirmed ~4.2 m (13.8 ft). Females grow larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — severely depleted; NW Atlantic population estimated at <10% of historical levels

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 3–14 pups per litter (avg. ~6–10); 22–24 month gestation; breeds every 3 years

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Bony fish, smaller sharks and rays, squid, octopus, crustaceans

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Extremely low — the combination of 20-year maturity, triennial breeding, and long gestation gives one of the lowest intrinsic growth rates of any shark

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Long-distance coastal migration (up to 3,200 km recorded); broad diet generalism allows exploitation of diverse prey assemblages across habitats

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and warm temperate coastal and offshore waters worldwide — Atlantic, Indian, and Pacific Oceans; absent from the central Pacific

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: 0–400 m (0–1,312 ft); most common in coastal shelf waters to 200 m

threats.name: Overfishing
threats.description: Heavily targeted in directed US Atlantic fisheries from the 1960s through the 1990s, leading to a population collapse estimated at 70–80% in the Northwest Atlantic. US restrictions imposed in 2000 have not produced measurable recovery due to the species' extremely slow reproductive rate.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Among the most commonly caught large sharks in pelagic longlines and coastal gillnets globally. High juvenile bycatch in inshore nursery habitats is especially damaging given the species' slow maturation timeline.

threats.name: Finning
threats.description: Dusky shark fins are highly valued in the international fin trade, with large individuals producing particularly prized fins. Finning continues in regions without robust enforcement.

threats.name: Extremely Slow Reproduction
threats.description: With maturity at ~20 years, a 2-year gestation, and triennial breeding cycles, the dusky shark has one of the lowest population growth rates of any shark — making even low levels of ongoing mortality unsustainable.

threats.name: Nursery Habitat Loss
threats.description: Juveniles rely on shallow coastal and estuarine nursery areas that are subject to development, pollution, and habitat degradation. Disruption of nursery grounds compounds the effects of direct fishing mortality.

actionItems.title: Support US Atlantic Recovery Measures
actionItems.description: The Northwest Atlantic dusky shark population has been under a no-retention rule since 2000 yet shows little sign of recovery. Advocate for continued protection and stronger bycatch mitigation requirements in longline fisheries that incidentally catch dusky sharks.

actionItems.title: Protect Coastal Nursery Habitat
actionItems.description: Dusky shark juveniles depend on specific shallow coastal and estuarine nursery grounds. Support marine spatial planning and coastal development restrictions that preserve these critical areas.

actionItems.title: Advocate for CITES Appendix II Enforcement
actionItems.description: Dusky sharks are listed on CITES Appendix II. Push for stricter species-level identification and trade documentation to prevent fins from being exported under misidentified labels.

actionItems.title: Donate to Shark Conservation
actionItems.description: Support the Shark Trust and IUCN SSG programs that monitor dusky shark bycatch, fund tagging research tracking migration and nursery use, and advocate for binding international catch limits.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — NW Atlantic at <10% historical levels; no recovery observed

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — open-ocean range intact; coastal nursery habitat degrading

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — coastal and pelagic fish stocks heavily exploited across its range

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — no-retention in US Atlantic; bycatch and targeting continues globally

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Limited — US no-retention since 2000; CITES II; no global binding measures

populationTrendMeta.note: Rough global estimates modeled from documented NW Atlantic collapse (70–80% decline by 2000) and extrapolated to other ocean basins where assessments are absent. No global population census exists. Figures carry high uncertainty.

preyDeclineRegions.name: NW Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal fish stocks heavily fished; prey assemblage significantly altered

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal and pelagic prey species severely depleted by trawling and longlining

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef and pelagic fish stocks under intense pressure across the region

preyDeclineRegions.name: W. Africa
preyDeclineRegions.note: Artisanal and industrial fisheries have heavily depleted coastal prey fish

preyDeclineRegions.name: Australasia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Moderate fishing pressure; some key prey stocks managed

fishingPressureRegions.name: NW Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: No-retention rule since 2000; high bycatch in tuna and swordfish longlines continues

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted and bycatch mortality; no species-specific protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: W. Africa
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted by artisanal and industrial fleets for fins and meat; minimal oversight

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavy coastal and pelagic fishing effort; dusky sharks common bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australasia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Some managed fisheries; bycatch in longlines and gillnets ongoing
